nativeRange.habitat: Reef slopes, back-reef flats, lagoonal and turbid habitats; encrusts hard substrate from shallow zones to moderate depth

decorPreferences: stable rockwork with open surface area for encrusting
avoid placement adjacent to aggressive LPS or sweeper-tentacle corals
allow growth room — fast encrusting spreader

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Supplemental feeding of rotifers, amino acids, or small zooplankton is optional but has been reported to enhance growth and coloration. Captures dissolved organics and small particulates from the water column.

goodWith: other SPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: LPS corals with long sweeper tentacles placed in direct contact
corals placed directly in its projected growth path

summary: Stylocoeniella is a rare, encrusting SPS coral in family Astrocoeniidae. Tightly-packed small polyps give colonies a velvety texture; often confused with Montipora or Porites. Hardy for an SPS; moderate-high lighting and flow.
careNotes:
Stylocoeniella belongs to the small and uncommonly kept family Astrocoeniidae — most aquarists encounter Acropora, Montipora, and Porites long before this genus. In the aquarium trade it is sometimes listed simply as 'Thorn Coral' or traded under designer-morph names such as 'Electric Daisy'. It is not frequently imported directly from the wild; most available specimens are aquacultured frags that circulate within the hobby.

Identification confusion: In low-to-moderate flow, Stylocoeniella polyps extend and the colony resembles certain Montipora or small-polyp Porites. In high flow polyps retract and the encrusting sheet may be taken for Montipora. Small frags have been documented being mislabeled as Montipora in the trade. Close inspection under a loupe reveals distinct thorn-like coenosteum spines (giving rise to the 'Thorn Coral' common name) that are absent in Montipora and Porites. The family Astrocoeniidae contains only a handful of aquarium-relevant genera (Stylocoeniella, Palauastrea), making it one of the rarer coral families represented in reef tanks.

Lighting: Generally advised in the moderate-to-high range (~150–300 PAR). It is more tolerant of lower light than typical SPS and has been documented thriving in the 50–100 PAR range on tank walls or in partially shaded spots (Tidal Gardens). Sudden exposure to high PAR tends to bleach frags; acclimate gradually. Brown coloration usually indicates insufficient light.

Flow: Moderate to moderately strong flow is generally recommended — enough to prevent detritus accumulation on the encrusting surface without directly blasting tissue. Gentle alternating flow from a wavemaker is preferred over direct laminar flow.

Growth: Fast-growing for an SPS. A small frag can encrust and spread across significant rockwork area within months under good conditions. Plan placement carefully — once established it is difficult to remove without damaging the host rock.

Disease resistance: Reportedly more resistant to Slow and Rapid Tissue Necrosis (STN/RTN) than many other SPS corals, which contributes to its relative ease of care despite being an SPS.

Water chemistry: Tolerates a broader nutrient range than more demanding SPS (Acropora). Nitrate 5–10 ppm and phosphate 0.05–0.1 ppm are generally recommended, but sources indicate tolerance up to 50 ppm nitrate and 2.5 ppm phosphate (Tidal Gardens). Calcium ~400–450 ppm, kH 7–11 dKH, magnesium ~1300–1400 ppm.

Fragging: Very easy to frag; encrusting base can be cut with a bandsaw or tile saw and frags readily attach to plugs or rubble. This is the primary reason it became more commercially available despite infrequent wild import.
breedingNotes: Fragging is the standard propagation method and is straightforward due to the encrusting growth form. A section of substrate with attached coral can be cut, glued to a frag plug, and expected to recover and grow. Sexual reproduction in the wild occurs via broadcast spawning; deliberate captive sexual breeding is not practiced.

primary.name: ReefChasers – Stylocoeniella Coral Care Guide
primary.notes: Primary source for PAR (150 moderate), kH (8-9 dKH), calcium (400-450), care level (beginner), temperature (82°F / ~28°C natural), family (Astrocoeniidae), feeding, native range (Indo-Pacific).

additional.name: Tidal Gardens – Stylocoeniella Coral Care
additional.notes: Cross-checked: flow (low-medium), lighting (50-100 PAR lower bound), calcium (~425 ppm), alkalinity (7-11 dkH), magnesium (~1350 ppm), native range (Indonesia and Australia), confusion with Montipora. Recommended lower PAR than other sources — averaged/reconciled toward 150-300 range with note of low-end tolerance.

additional.name: Whitlyn Aquatics – Stylocoeniella Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Cross-checked: temperature (74-80°F = 23-27°C), pH (8.1-8.4), salinity (1.024-1.026), lighting (100-250 PAR), flow (moderate to strong), placement (rockwork with open space), confusion with Montipora and Goniopora noted.

additional.name: Frag Box – WWC Electric Daisy Stylocoeniella
additional.notes: Cross-checked: placement (middle to high), lighting (moderate to high), flow (moderate to high), native range (Indonesia and Great Barrier Reef), ease of fragging confirmed.

additional.name: WoRMS – Stylocoeniella guentheri
additional.notes: Taxonomy confirmation: Order Scleractinia, Family Astrocoeniidae. Depth range 5-50 m. Distribution: Indo-Pacific including Australia, French Polynesia, Guam, Maldives, South China Sea.
